item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Making the reader feel like part of the anomaly was one of my goals on writing this article, so I'm really happy to hear that! Thanks for liking it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked the article, made me feel like I was a part of the anomaly and the culture by starting to decode it. Its cool how the foot notes taper off and you are actually able to read it with the flowers and all.
Def +1 loved it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I really worked hard making those pixel arts so I'm glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed it!
And yes the last letter is hard to read. Bellow is the spoiler.
+ show block
– hide block

First of all, congratulations on the promotion.

From today, you are the head researcher of SCP-4893, and thus SCP-4893 itself. It'll be very difficult to read this letter, but you'll get used to it soon.

Well, you might be wondering. Why do I have to become SCP-4893? Why not use D-Class personnel?

It was like that at first when SCP-4893 was discovered. In fact, SCP-4893 has been inherited for many years by healthy, young D-Class personnel. But there's something very different now: the literacy rate. Now many people can read and write texts in developed and underdeveloped countries. Inevitably, the possibility of exposure to SCP-4893's capabilities has increased. Any textual medium written or typed by SCP-4893 is a source of infection. There have been cases in which a D-Class personnel who have followed SCP-4893 in the past carved words on the wall and made many of the staff become SCP-4893.

Our philosophy is "Secure, Contain, Protect". It is not good enough to just secure and contain any SCP. It is therefore best to choose SCP-4893's successors from among Foundation personnel who can understand the Foundation's philosophy and carry it out. Inherit what we have contained and protected. And someday you will entrust the mission to someone else. Good luck.

Former SCP-4893 Head Researcher

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really creative. And the fact that you created all the pixel art shows how much effort you put into this. It’s great! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last thing is kinda hard to read but overall I enjoyed the gimmick. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for reading!

At first I wanted to use photos of the real flowers, but since I couldn't find all of the flower's photo licensed under CC BY-SA 3.0 I decided to produce the synesthesia in pixel art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you liked it!

It is true it's a little hard to keep track of the meanings. I might incorporate your idea to make the article more reader friendly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed it :)

I readacted the second half of the years because I was able to find the specific years and only centuries. But I do agree that they are rather disturbing, so I revised the years based on your suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dug the article, and gets a +1 from me, just starting there :)

I did feel that always redacting the second half of the years (esp when it's in the 1500s and such) was unnecessary and only added unneeded visual clutter. We already know something happened in 15XX, why does the specific year need to be hidden? How is that dangerous information? Why is the Foundation being so coy and tease-y? If it was 1524, just say it. It doesn't add any extra mystery or what-have-you wondering whether it was 1543, 1508, or 1533 instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It also doesn't help that the style evokes emojipasta memes more so than flower imagery, though that might be more blamed on internet culture rotting my brain than the SCP itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Call me a grouch, but I'm not a fan of the flowery pixel art. It's really distracting and I feel it could've been executed better than this. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I'm a sucker for Language of Flowers references, and this is a nice short punchy article with an unexpected format screw, thus you have my +1.

I will say that following the meanings is a bit difficult when there are so many flowers to keep track of. What I would have recommended is that the filenames include both the flower and the "meaning", so if the reader happens to have forgotten what one meaning was, they could just hover over the image to see its url and not need to search through the entire document for the first footnote. That's just a quality of life edit that might not be entirely necessary, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All the pixel art of the flowers are made by me.
Special thanks to FeS_ryuukatetu for helping me with the syntax!
